sections.whoTheyAre: The Army National Guard is the state-based reserve component of the U.S. Army, serving under both state governors and federal authority. A Title 32 position is a federal role tied to National Guard support, typically a civilian or dual-status technician job. This posting is based in Raleigh, North Carolina; confirm on USAJOBS whether it is a dual-status technician role (which may require Guard membership) or a straight federal civilian position, because the requirements differ.
sections.whatTheRoleOwns: A Logistics Management Specialist manages the flow, accountability, and readiness of supplies, equipment, and property for the unit. Day to day this usually means tracking inventory, coordinating maintenance and resupply, maintaining accurate property records, and preparing reports that leaders use to make readiness decisions. Expect heavy use of Army supply systems (such as GCSS-Army) and adherence to federal property and funding regulations.
sections.whyYourRecordFits: Your record shows direct property accountability: you maintained 100% accountability of equipment valued at over $500,000 with no losses, which is the core habit this role rewards. Your experience planning and carrying out operations and training demonstrates coordinating people and resources against a schedule. Supervising and training up to 12 people shows you can be relied on for records and standards others depend on.
sections.likelyQuestions: Expect questions like: Describe a time you accounted for or reconciled equipment or inventory, and what specific method or record you used. What logistics or supply software have you worked with, and to what depth. Tell us about a time a shortage or discrepancy occurred and what you did. A strong answer names a verifiable specific, an actual count, dollar figure, timeline, or system, rather than claiming you are organized or detail-oriented.
sections.honestGap: The clearest gap is federal logistics systems and regulations: your record shows hands-on accountability but not documented use of Army supply software like GCSS-Army or civilian logistics platforms. Address it plainly by naming the property systems and processes you did use in the Marine Corps, describing how you tracked that $500,000 in equipment, and stating your readiness to certify on the unit's system quickly, without claiming experience you do not have.
